content:
All files in the archive carry timestamps or version numbers in their names as per the naming convention (detailed in the next section), which makes them immutable records – once written, a file isn’t edited but rather a new version file is created for changes. Cross-linking is done explicitly in text: for example, a session log might end with “(see World Alpha archive entry WAVE-STORY-LOG-ALPHA-v0_2)” or we might use a simple reference syntax to point to IDs of related artifacts. By keeping these references human-readable (and machine-parsable), any AI agent can follow the chain: it can open the session index to find something, then jump to a world file, from there follow a reference to a character file, and so on – essentially traversing a graph of knowledge that lives in plain files. We avoid opaque identifiers; everything is part of the **narrative graph**.

To help navigation, each archive subdirectory has a manifest (index) that is itself a simple text table or list. We might format manifest lines as CSV or Markdown table rows, e.g.:

```
ID, Title, Date, Tags, Path
WORLD-ALPHA, "World Alpha Genesis", 2025-11-23, myth, worlds/World-Alpha/World-Alpha-genesis.md
context: From document: # Blueprint for a Self-Evolving Creative Operating System — chunk 24 (chunk 24/38)
